comments.author: @evelynkwagala9732
comments.text: Thanks for the history of River Nile -  very impressive. But then the drone footage you're showing with round grass thatched houses in a semi-arid area is not the Buganda you're talking about.  And the massive grass thatched structure where former kings were buried is not any of these little ones you're showing. Also, the market you're presenting as Owino is not that one. The one you're showing is most likely not in Uganda. This needed more work in order to present things more accurately. Don't be lazy next time.

comments.author: @BestHiddenPlaces
comments.text: I honestly never knew the Nile began in Uganda. Learned something new today 🥰

comments.author: @abcdjkx
comments.text: The chihclid speciation is not really a case of evolution as no new genetic material was created, rather pre-existing material was selected for or selected out to produce specialization.  One fish didn't pontaneoiusly grow big jaws, it simply retained them while others did not. What this does show is that adaptation need not take "millions of years" as claimed but rather a relatively few generations (I don't accept the 14000 years date, it is more recent than that).

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: That's a solid clarification — you're right that speciation through selection on existing genetic variation is a different mechanism than novel mutation, and it's a fair point that rapid adaptive radiation (cichlids are a classic case) can happen much faster than "millions of years" implies. Appreciate the precision.

comments.author: @lebohang8405
comments.text: I've watched multiple documentaries of people claiming to have found the source of the Nile. Most of those would point to a spring or trickles in in the mountains somewhere upstream of tributaries flowing into Lake Victoria. Then they'd somehow connect one to of those to the outlet that forms the true Nile flowing out in the North called the white Nile. My question is how many rivers flow into Lake Victoria? How on earth can one single out one of those to be a sole or primary source of the Nile. Logically id agree that they are the source of Lake Victoria at the very least and with that logic then suggest that the lake be the only sole source of the White Nile. My humble opinion

replies.author: @lebohang8405
replies.text: ​@WhereonEarth4KThank you. Just clarify my position. Why is it that with the Blue Nile the identification of the source different? It's clearly determined to be Lake Tana and yet with the White a different methodology seems to apply

replies.author: @WhereonEarth4K
replies.text: Fair challenge — dozens of rivers and streams feed Lake Victoria, so "the source of the Nile" is really shorthand for "the point where the lake's outflow becomes a defined river." Different explorers have championed different specific tributaries as the "true" source over the centuries precisely because the lake itself doesn't have one single feeding stream — it's genuinely more accurate to think of the lake as the primary source, as you say.
